clean_story:
Vibrations as tools. Sound as a weapon.

"Resonance" means matching vibrations—like pushing a swing at just the right moment to make it fly higher. Animals exploit this physics trick to hunt, communicate, and even build.

A male mosquito’s wings hum at 600 beats per second, a pitch female mosquitoes must hear to mate. Their antennae are tuned like radio receivers, vibrating only at that exact frequency—a love song no other species detects. Tiger moths do the opposite: they jam bat sonar. When a bat’s echolocation clicks near 50,000 hertz, the moth’s wings emit their own 50,000-hertz bursts, creating a "phantom echo" that confuses the predator like a fake GPS signal.

Some animals build with resonance. Termites construct mounds with arches that resonate at 100 hertz—the same frequency as their warning head-bangs. A single tap travels through the walls like a telegraph, alerting the colony faster than scent or sight. Even spider webs act as resonant filters: when a fly struggles, its buzz vibrates the silk at a unique pitch, telling the spider exactly where lunch landed.

Three related subjects are echoes in bat hunting, insects’ ultrasonic wars, and how humans copy animal acoustics.
